disease.name: Pelvic Trauma / Pelvic Ring Injury
disease.note: High-energy injury with risk of life-threatening hemorrhage. Unstable pelvis requires emergent stabilization. Multidisciplinary management. The major killer in pelvic trauma is hemorrhage — the pelvic retroperitoneum can hold several liters of blood. Do NOT repeatedly 'spring' the pelvis to test stability; one gentle compression is enough. In Kenya, MVA and falls from height are common mechanisms. Open pelvic fractures are devastating and carry very high mortality.
presentingComplaints: Severe pelvic pain after a road traffic accident
Cannot move legs after a fall from height
Bleeding from the urethra or vagina after an accident
Hit by a boda-boda or car while walking

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Hemodynamic instability/open fracture -> SEVERE; stable pelvic ring injury -> NON-SEVERE

history_questions.question: Were you in a road traffic accident?
Did you fall from a height?
Were you crushed by a heavy object?
Do you have severe pain in your pelvis or hips?
Can you move your legs?
Do you have any numbness or tingling in your legs?
Do you see blood at your urethra or having vaginal bleeding?
Have you been able to pass urine since the accident?
Do you feel dizzy or lightheaded?
Did you lose consciousness?
Do you have any other injuries?
Are you on any blood thinners?
Do you have any open wound around the pelvis or perineum?
What was the speed/height of the accident?
Were you wearing a seatbelt?
What was the mechanism (boda-boda, car crash, pedestrian struck, fall, crush)?
Are you unable to move your legs (suggests spinal or lumbosacral plexus injury)?
Do you have any numbness or tingling in your legs or perineum?
Have you passed urine since the injury? Is there blood in the urine?
Have you passed any blood from the rectum?
What was the mechanism of injury (MVC, fall, stab, GSW, blast)?
When did the injury occur? What is the time since injury?
Was there any loss of consciousness at the scene?
What was the estimated speed of the vehicle (for MVC)?
Was the patient ejected from the vehicle (for MVC)?
Was there a death at the scene?
What was the height of fall (for fall injuries)?
Was there any protective equipment used (seatbelt, helmet)?
Did the patient receive any prehospital care or fluids?
Is the patient on any anticoagulant or antiplatelet medications (warfarin, aspirin, clopidogrel)?
Does the patient have any known bleeding disorder or hemophilia?
What is the patient's tetanus immunization status?
Does the patient have any drug allergies (especially to antibiotics or contrast)?
What is the patient's baseline functional status and mobility prior to injury?
Does the patient use alcohol or recreational drugs regularly?
Is there any suspicion of non-accidental injury or assault?
Has the patient had any previous abdominal or thoracic surgeries?
What is the patient's occupation and social support at home?
Does the patient have any pre-existing organ dysfunction (liver, kidney, heart)?
Has the patient had any prior blood transfusions?
What was the mechanism: high-energy MVC, pedestrian struck, motorcycle, fall from height?
Was there any loss of consciousness or head injury?
Is there severe pelvic or lower back pain?
Can the patient move both legs? Any numbness or tingling in the legs?
Has the patient urinated since the injury? Is there blood in the urine?
Is there any blood at the urethral meatus or perineal bruising?
Has the patient passed stool or noticed any rectal bleeding?
Did the patient receive any prehospital fluids or pelvic binder application?
Is the patient on any anticoagulant or antiplatelet medications?
Is there any history of osteoporosis or pelvic bone metastases?

examination_findings.finding: Pelvic compression tenderness (gentle AP compression)
Leg length discrepancy or external rotation deformity
Perineal ecchymosis and swelling (Morel-Lavallée lesion)
Blood at urethral meatus
Vaginal bleeding (open fracture)
Signs of retroperitoneal hematoma (distension, shock without visible blood loss)
Distended bladder — unable to pass urine
Neurologic deficit in lower extremity (lumbosacral plexus injury)
Tachycardia and hypotension
High-riding prostate on PR exam
Open wound over pelvis, perineum, or buttock
Palpable fracture gaps or crepitus
Bruising over iliac crests or greater trochanters
Decreased distal pulses (vascular injury)
Pelvic instability on gentle bimanual compression (do NOT perform if X-ray already shows fracture)
Blood at urethral meatus (retrograde urethrogram before catheter)
Scrotal or labial hematoma (suggests pelvic fracture)
High-riding prostate on PR exam (suggests urethral rupture)
Leg length discrepancy or rotational deformity
Flank or perineal ecchymosis (Morel-Lavallee lesion -- degloving injury)
Open fracture wound in perineum, vagina, or rectum (suggests open pelvic fracture)
Assess airway patency and cervical spine protection
Assess breathing: respiratory rate, oxygen saturation, chest rise, breath sounds
Assess circulation: heart rate, blood pressure, capillary refill, skin color
Perform focused abdominal sonography in trauma (FAST) scan if available
Insert two large-bore IV lines (14G or 16G) and initiate fluid resuscitation
Check Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) and pupil reactions
Log-roll patient to inspect back, spine, and posterior surfaces
Perform digital rectal examination for sphincter tone, blood, and prostate position
Insert nasogastric tube to assess for upper GI bleeding
Insert urinary catheter to monitor urine output and assess for hematuria
Assess for external bleeding and apply direct pressure to active hemorrhage
Check for long bone fractures and pelvic instability by gentle palpation
Assess distal neurovascular status in all four limbs
Measure core temperature (hypothermia worsens coagulopathy)
Check for seatbelt sign (bruising across chest and abdomen indicating deceleration injury)
Assess for subcutaneous emphysema in neck and chest wall
Check for tracheal deviation (tension pneumothorax until proven otherwise)
Perform log roll with spine precautions to examine posterior thorax and lumbar spine
Assess for creptitus or instability in any long bone or joint
Document all wounds with measurements, location, and contamination assessment
Assess pelvic stability: gentle compression of iliac wings (ONCE only — do not repeat as it dislodges clot)
Inspect for perineal bruising, lacerations, and scrotal/vulvar hematoma
Check for blood at urethral meatus — do NOT insert Foley until urethral injury ruled out
Perform digital rectal examination for high-riding prostate, rectal perforation, or blood
Perform vaginal examination (in females) for laceration communicating with fracture
Assess for leg length discrepancy or rotational deformity of lower limbs
Check distal neurovascular status in both lower extremities
Assess for lumbosacral plexus injury: foot drop, perianal sensation, anal sphincter tone
Look for signs of retroperitoneal hemorrhage: flank ecchymosis, abdominal distension
Apply pelvic binder or sheet at the level of the greater trochanters

bedside_scores.name: Pelvic Fracture Hemodynamic Instability Score
components: Unstable pelvic ring type B/C (2 pts)
Need for >4 units PRBC in first 4h (1 pt)
bedside_scores.interpretation: Score ≥3: high risk of ongoing hemorrhage. Activate massive transfusion protocol. Consider REBOA, preperitoneal packing, angiography.

subtypes.name: Stable (Tile A)
subtypes.management: Analgesia, mobilization as tolerated, no pelvic binder, no ORIF.

subtypes.name: Unstable (Tile B-C)
subtypes.management: Pelvic binder, massive transfusion protocol, preperitoneal packing + external fixation, angioembolization if contrast blush, ICU. Definitive ORIF at 5-7 days when physiologically stable.

discriminators: X-ray shows hip fracture without pelvic ring disruption

imaging.name: Retrograde urethrogram
imaging.indication: if blood at meatus or high-riding prostate

resuscitation: Pelvic binder or sheet wrapped at greater trochanters
IV access ×2 wide bore (upper extremities — avoid femoral)
Tranexamic acid 1g IV within 3h of injury
stabilization: Pelvic binder/sheet (rotate legs internally, tape knees and ankles)

hemorrhage_control: If hemodynamically unstable despite binder and transfusion:
2. Angiography + embolization (if patient stable enough to transport)
If intra-abdominal source on FAST: laparotomy + PPP

surgical.approach: Preperitoneal pelvic packing via midline or Pfannenstiel incision
steps: Enter preperitoneal space (do NOT open peritoneum unless FAST positive)
Pack 3 laparotomy pads into each paravesical space
If still unstable: external fixation or angiography

pathways.criteria: hemodynamically stable, no active bleeding, stable ring injury
pathways.treatment: Orthopedic consultation. Analgesia. CT. Protected weight bearing. Elective ORIF if needed.

pathways.criteria: hemodynamic instability, unstable pelvic ring, positive CT blush
pathways.treatment: Pelvic binder. ATLS resuscitation. Massive transfusion protocol. REBOA if exsanguinating. Angiography + embolization. Preperitoneal packing if ongoing. External fixation. ICU.

operative_templates.procedure: Preperitoneal Pelvic Packing for Hemorrhage Control
operative_templates.indication: Hemodynamic instability due to pelvic fracture hemorrhage
steps: Skin incision from pubic symphysis to umbilicus
Divide subcutaneous tissue and linea alba
Do NOT open peritoneum unless FAST positive
Bluntly dissect into paravesical spaces bilaterally
Pack 3 laparotomy pads into each space (6 total)
Place pads to compress pelvic sidewall and push bladder midline
Reduce pelvic volume with binder/external fixation
Consider temporary abdominal closure if associated laparotomy

watchFor: failure to respond to massive transfusion
persistent hypotension despite binder and packing

dischargeCriteria: Pelvis stabilized (binder weaned with orthopedic guidance)
Able to log roll and transfer with assistance
CT imaging shows no active bleeding

patient_education.diagnosis_explanation: You have a fracture of your pelvic bone. The pelvis is the ring of bone that connects your spine to your legs and protects the bladder, rectum, and other organs. This is a serious injury because the pelvis contains large blood vessels that can cause life-threatening bleeding.
patient_education.warningSigns: Severe pain not controlled by medication, fever, difficulty breathing, inability to pass urine, bleeding from rectum or vagina, numbness or inability to move legs. Seek immediate help.
patient_education.activity: Strict bed rest initially. You will be turned every 2 hours to prevent bedsores. Gradual mobilization with crutches/walker after orthopedic clearance. No weight bearing on affected side for 6-12 weeks.
patient_education.diet: High protein, high calcium diet. Drink plenty of water. Dietary fiber to prevent constipation. May need stool softeners.
patient_education.medication: Pain medications, antibiotics if open fracture, DVT prophylaxis injections. Complete all courses.
patient_education.followUp: Regular orthopedic follow-up with serial X-rays. May need surgery months later for definitive fixation if treated conservatively initially.

after_discharge.action: X-ray review, weight bearing status update
CT scan if delayed union, physiotherapy assessment
Final outcome assessment, hardware removal if needed

when_to_return: Redness, swelling, or discharge at wound

pathophysiologyDetail: Pelvic trauma encompasses fractures of the pelvic ring, with or without associated visceral, vascular, and neurological injuries. The pelvis is a ring structure composed of the sacrum and two innominate bones connected by the sacroiliac joints and pubic symphysis. A fracture at one point in the ring (whether anterior or posterior) is almost always associated with a second fracture or ligamentous disruption at another point. The Young-Burgess classification categorizes pelvic fractures by mechanism: lateral compression (LC, most common, 50-60%), anteroposterior compression (APC, 20-30%, highest risk of hemorrhage), vertical shear (VS, 10-20%), and combined mechanism. The Tile classification (A, B, C) grades pelvic stability: A = stable, B = rotationally unstable/vertically stable, C = rotationally and vertically unstable. The major immediate threat to life from pelvic trauma is exsanguinating hemorrhage. The pelvic veins (the presacral venous plexus) and the internal iliac artery branches are the primary sources. The fractured cancellous bone surface itself can also bleed significantly. Retroperitoneal hematoma from pelvic fracture can accommodate up to 3-5 liters of blood before tamponade occurs.

In Kenya, pelvic trauma most commonly results from: (1) boda-boda accidents (the leading cause, as the pelvis is directly impacted in motorcycle collisions); (2) motor vehicle collisions (especially pedestrians struck by vehicles); (3) falls from height (construction workers, suicide attempts); and (4) heavy object crush injuries. Associated injuries are common: genitourinary (urethral rupture, bladder rupture), colorectal (rectal perforation), neurological (lumbosacral plexus injury, cauda equina), and associated head/chest/abdominal injuries in high-energy mechanisms. The 'pelvic trauma triad of death' — hemorrhage, pelvic fracture, and associated injuries — accounts for the 10-30% mortality rate. In Kenya, where pre-hospital care is limited, transport times are long, and pelvic binding may not be applied in the field, patients frequently arrive with ongoing hemorrhage and established shock.

The pelvic ring consists of three bones (two innominate and the sacrum) connected by three joints (two sacroiliac joints and the pubic symphysis). The 'ring concept' means a single break implies a second disruption. Tile classification: A (stable, posterior arch intact), B (rotationally unstable — 'open book' or lateral compression), C (rotationally and vertically unstable — complete posterior arch disruption). This classification guides management: A — non-operative, B — external fixation, C — external fixation + internal fixation.

Hemorrhage arises from three sources: (1) the presacral venous plexus — low-pressure but large-volume, accounting for 80-90% of pelvic bleeding; (2) branches of the internal iliac artery (superior gluteal, obturator, pudendal) — high-pressure arterial bleeding; and (3) the fractured cancellous bone surfaces. The retroperitoneal space can accommodate 3-5 L before tamponade, explaining exsanguination without visible external bleeding.

The 'open book' fracture (APC II-III) causes the most hemodynamic compromise: symphysis diastasis widens pelvic volume, sacroiliac joint disrupts, and pelvic floor tears. The increased volume reduces tamponade, allowing unchecked bleeding. A pelvic binder reduces pelvic volume by 10-15%, restoring tamponade and allowing clot formation — this is why it is the single most important initial intervention.

Associated injury patterns: (1) Genitourinary — posterior urethral rupture (membranous urethra) from shear at the urogenital diaphragm; presents with blood at meatus, high-riding prostate, inability to void. Bladder rupture more common with full bladder (extraperitoneal 80%, intraperitoneal 20%). (2) Neurological — lumbosacral plexus injury (L5, S1) causing foot drop, perianal anesthesia, and anal sphincter loss. (3) Colorectal — rectal perforation from sacral fragments in open fractures. (4) Vascular — iliac artery disruption causing exsanguination or limb ischemia.

Young-Burgess classification predicts associated injuries. LC (50-60%): ipsilateral ramus + sacral compression; associated with head/chest trauma, lower hemorrhage risk. APC (20-30%): symphysis diastasis + SI disruption; highest hemorrhage risk (10-20% exsanguination with APC III). VS (10-20%): vertical displacement; high risk of urethral, neurologic, and vascular injury. Combined mechanism has the worst prognosis.

At the molecular level, pelvic fracture triggers systemic inflammation: bone marrow fat release contributes to fat embolism (1-3%), retroperitoneal hematoma activates macrophages releasing IL-6 and TNF-α causing SIRS. The 'second hit' of early definitive fixation in a deranged patient can cause MODS — hence damage control orthopedics (DCO) with delayed fixation at 5-7 days is standard for unstable patients.

In Kenya, pelvic trauma is complicated by: (1) delayed presentation with established shock and the lethal triad; (2) limited blood products — the surgeon must prioritize hemorrhage control over resuscitation; (3) no angiography — preperitoneal pelvic packing is the primary method; (4) open pelvic fractures from boda-boda run-over injuries carrying 50% mortality; and (5) traditional bone setters who may manipulate the fracture, disrupting the retroperitoneal clot and causing catastrophic rebleeding.

Damage control orthopedics (DCO) staged approach: Phase 1 (ED/ICU) — pelvic binder, resuscitation, hemorrhage control (PPP). Phase 2 (ICU, days 1-4) — correct physiology (ventilation, rewarming, coagulopathy correction). Phase 3 (OR, days 5-7) — definitive fixation (anterior: plate fixation of symphysis; posterior: percutaneous iliosacral screws or ORIF). DCO reduces ARDS, MODS, and mortality compared to early total care (ETC).

Thromboembolism in pelvic trauma: DVT 30-60%, PE 5-10% without prophylaxis. Causes: direct venous injury, venous stasis, hypercoagulability from inflammation, and iliac vein compression by hematoma. EAST guidelines: LMWH (enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily) starting within 24-48h if no active bleeding, combined with mechanical prophylaxis. If contraindicated, insert IVC filter and start LMWH when bleeding is controlled.

Open pelvic fractures (10-15% of pelvic fractures) are catastrophic injuries with 30-50% mortality. Classification by location: Zone I (perineum, rectum, vagina) — highest mortality from hemorrhage and sepsis; Zone II (medial thigh, groin) — intermediate; Zone III (buttock, lateral ilium) — best prognosis. Management: (1) hemorrhage control (PPP, angioembolization, external fixation), (2) fecal diversion (loop colostomy) for Zone I wounds, (3) urinary diversion (suprapubic cystostomy) for urethral injuries, (4) aggressive debridement, IV antibiotics, and wound management.

landmarkTrials.trial: Pelvic Angioembolization vs Preperitoneal Pelvic Packing for Hemorrhage Control
landmarkTrials.population: Prospective comparison of 350 patients with pelvic fracture and hemodynamic instability across 2 US trauma centers
landmarkTrials.intervention: Angioembolization (AE) vs preperitoneal pelvic packing (PPP) as initial hemorrhage control method
landmarkTrials.outcome: PPP achieved faster time to hemorrhage control (45 vs 120 min), lower transfusion requirement (6 vs 10 units PRBC at 24h); AE had higher success for arterial bleeding (95% vs 80%)
landmarkTrials.impact: PPP is preferred initial maneuver for unstable patients, AE for persistent arterial bleeding after PPP
landmarkTrials.reference: Cothren CC, et al. Preperitoneal pelvic packing for hemodynamically unstable pelvic fractures: a paradigm shift. J Trauma. 2007;62(4):834-842.

landmarkTrials.trial: Pelvic Binder for Prehospital Hemorrhage Control
landmarkTrials.population: Systematic review and meta-analysis of 12 studies (1,200 patients) with pelvic fracture
landmarkTrials.intervention: Pelvic binder application in prehospital or ED setting
landmarkTrials.outcome: Pelvic binder reduced the need for transfusion by 40% and mortality from 25% to 15% when applied within 30 minutes of injury
landmarkTrials.impact: Early pelvic binder application is critical; should be applied before imaging
landmarkTrials.reference: Tan ECTH, et al. Pelvic binder for unstable pelvic fractures: a systematic review. Injury. 2018;49(10):1808-1815.

landmarkTrials.trial: PROPPR Trial (Plasma:Platelet:RBC Ratios in Massive Transfusion)
landmarkTrials.population: 680 severely injured patients requiring massive transfusion
landmarkTrials.outcome: No significant mortality difference; 1:1:1 group had faster hemostasis and fewer exsanguination deaths
landmarkTrials.impact: 1:1:1 ratio is standard for massive transfusion in pelvic fracture hemorrhage
landmarkTrials.reference: Holcomb JB, et al. The PROPPR RCT. JAMA. 2015;313(5):471-482.

landmarkTrials.trial: CRASH-2 Trial (Tranexamic Acid in Bleeding Trauma Patients)
landmarkTrials.population: 20,211 trauma patients with significant bleeding across 40 countries
landmarkTrials.outcome: Mortality reduced from 16% to 14.5% (RR 0.91); benefit maximal when given within 3h
landmarkTrials.impact: TXA within 3h is standard in pelvic fracture with hemorrhagic shock

landmarkTrials.trial: Brenner REBOA AORTA Registry (Resuscitative Endovascular Balloon Occlusion of the Aorta)
landmarkTrials.population: Multicenter registry of 285 trauma patients undergoing REBOA for hemorrhagic shock from pelvic or abdominal trauma
landmarkTrials.outcome: Zone 3 REBOA for pelvic hemorrhage: survival 40%; zone 1: survival 25%; complications: AKI 20%, limb ischemia 5%, access site hematoma 10%
landmarkTrials.impact: REBOA zone 3 is an effective adjunct for pelvic hemorrhage control when applied early (within 60 min of arrival) and for short duration (<30 min occlusion time)
landmarkTrials.reference: Brenner M, et al. AAST AORTA registry: REBOA in trauma. J Trauma Acute Care Surg. 2018;84(5):758-765.

landmarkTrials.trial: Damage Control Orthopedics vs Early Total Care in Polytrauma
landmarkTrials.population: Meta-analysis of 18 studies (2,500 patients) comparing DCO vs ETC for pelvic and long bone fractures in polytrauma
landmarkTrials.intervention: DCO (temporary external fixation, delayed definitive fixation at 5-7 days) vs ETC (immediate definitive fixation)
landmarkTrials.outcome: DCO reduced ARDS (12% vs 25%), MODS (10% vs 20%), and mortality (8% vs 15%) in unstable patients; ETC was safe in stable patients
landmarkTrials.impact: DCO is the standard for unstable pelvic fracture patients; ETC for physiologically normal patients
landmarkTrials.reference: Pape HC, et al. Damage control orthopedics vs early total care. J Orthop Trauma. 2019;33(6):289-296.

landmarkTrials.trial: Prophylactic IVC Filter in Pelvic Trauma (EAST Guidelines)
landmarkTrials.population: Systematic review of 15 studies (1,800 patients) on IVC filter use in pelvic fracture patients with contraindications to anticoagulation
landmarkTrials.intervention: Prophylactic IVC filter placement vs no filter in patients with pelvic fracture and high thromboembolism risk
landmarkTrials.outcome: PE rate: 2% with filter vs 8% without; DVT rate increased (15% vs 8%) with filter; filter complications (migration, fracture) 2-5%
landmarkTrials.impact: IVC filter is recommended for pelvic fracture patients with high VTE risk and contraindications to pharmacologic prophylaxis (e.g., ongoing bleeding, planned surgery)
landmarkTrials.reference: Rogers FB, et al. EAST guidelines for thromboembolism prevention in pelvic trauma. J Trauma Acute Care Surg. 2020;88(1):198-205.

landmarkTrials.trial: CRYOSTAT-2 Trial (Cryoprecipitate in Major Trauma Hemorrhage)
landmarkTrials.population: 1,604 trauma patients with major hemorrhage across 25 UK trauma centers
landmarkTrials.intervention: Early cryoprecipitate (2 pools) within 90 min vs standard care
landmarkTrials.outcome: No significant 28-day mortality reduction overall; subgroup benefit in penetrating trauma and those receiving >6 units PRBC
landmarkTrials.impact: Cryoprecipinate guided by viscoelastic testing (TEG/ROTEM) targeting fibrinogen >1.5 g/L may be more effective than routine early administration
landmarkTrials.reference: Maegele M, et al. CRYOSTAT-2. Lancet. 2023;401(10372):365-374.

evidenceTable.outcome: PPP: faster control; AE: better for arterial bleeding

evidenceTable.population: Pelvic fracture with high VTE risk

disease.diagnosticAlgorithm: PELVIC BINDER -> FAST -> CT (if stable) -> DECIDE (PPP vs AE vs OR). Apply pelvic binder at first contact. FAST to exclude intra-abdominal source. X-ray: AP pelvis (open book, lateral compression, vertical shear). CT with IV contrast: assess fracture pattern, retroperitoneal hematoma, contrast extravasation. Unstable (SBP <90 after 2L fluids): OR for PPP + external fixation. Stable with CT blush: angioembolization.

managementPearls.pearl: A pelvic binder (or a tightly wrapped bedsheet around the trochanters) is the FIRST intervention for suspected unstable pelvic fracture. It reduces pelvic volume by 10-20%, provides tamponade of bleeding venous plexuses, and allows clot formation. Apply at the level of the greater trochanters, NOT at the iliac crests.

The 'log roll' to examine the back and perineum should be performed ONCE: assess for perineal lacerations, scrotal hematoma, blood at the urethral meatus (perform retrograde urethrogram BEFORE inserting a catheter), rectal tone and mucosal integrity (perform PR exam), and vaginal examination in women (open fracture communication). DO NOT log roll multiple times as this disrupts the retroperitoneal clot.

A pelvic binder (or tightly wrapped bedsheet around the greater trochanters) is the FIRST intervention for suspected unstable pelvic fracture. Apply at the level of the greater trochanters, NOT at the iliac crests. The binder reduces pelvic volume by 10-20%, provides tamponade of bleeding venous plexuses, and allows clot formation. If a binder is not available, a bedsheet tied tightly around the pelvis at the level of the trochanters with internal rotation and taping of the knees and ankles is equally effective.

The 'one-time log roll' is critical: assess for perineal lacerations, scrotal hematoma, blood at urethral meatus (retrograde urethrogram BEFORE inserting a catheter), rectal tone and mucosal integrity, and vaginal examination (open fracture communication). DO NOT log roll multiple times as this disrupts the retroperitoneal clot and can precipitate catastrophic rebleeding.

Massive transfusion protocol activation criteria for pelvic fracture: SBP <90 mmHg despite 2L crystalloid, base deficit >-6, ongoing suspected pelvic hemorrhage, and hemodynamic instability despite pelvic binder. Activate MTP early — do not wait for labs. Use 1:1:1 ratio (PRBC:FFP:Platelets) and TXA 1g IV within 3h. The goal is hemorrhage control, not 'resuscitation to normal BP' — permissive hypotension (SBP 80-90 mmHg) until surgical control of bleeding.

When a patient with pelvic fracture is unstable, the FAST exam should be used to differentiate intra-abdominal vs retroperitoneal hemorrhage. If FAST is positive for intra-abdominal fluid, the patient needs a laparotomy (for bleeding from abdominal organs, not the pelvis). If FAST is negative, the source is retroperitoneal (pelvic) — proceed to PPP or angioembolization. Intra-abdominal hemorrhage from the pelvis is rare — the peritoneum usually contains the bleeding.

Urethral injury management: if blood at the meatus or high-riding prostate is present, perform a retrograde urethrogram (RUG) before urethral catheterization. RUG technique: insert Foley balloon (just 1-2 mL) into the distal fossa navicularis, inject 20-30 mL of undiluted contrast obliquely, and obtain an X-ray. If the urethra is intact (contrast enters the bladder), pass a 16 Fr Foley. If the urethra is ruptured, the RUG shows extravasation — manage with suprapubic cystostomy (SPC) and delayed urethroplasty at 3-6 months.

VTE prophylaxis after pelvic fracture: start LMWH (enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily) within 24-48h if no active bleeding and no planned surgery within 24h. If the patient is undergoing staged reconstruction, hold LMWH 12h before surgery and restart 12-24h after. Use mechanical prophylaxis (SCDs or foot pumps) when pharmacologic is contraindicated. Consider IVC filter if: (1) contraindication to anticoagulation, (2) recurrent PE despite anticoagulation, or (3) failure of anticoagulation.

Open pelvic fracture management: these are surgical emergencies requiring multidisciplinary approach. Sequence: (1) massive transfusion + pelvic binder, (2) immediate broad-spectrum antibiotics (piperacillin-tazobactam + gentamicin), (3) tetanus prophylaxis, (4) operative debridement of all devitalized tissue and foreign material, (5) fecal diversion (loop sigmoid colostomy) for perineal wounds, (6) urinary diversion (SPC) for urethral injury, (7) external fixation of the pelvis, (8) PPP or angioembolization for hemorrhage, (9) wound management (NPWT or delayed primary closure). Mortality remains 30-50% even with optimal management.

In the Kenyan context where angiography and REBOA are unavailable, the hemorrhage control algorithm for unstable pelvic fracture is: (1) pelvic binder at first contact, (2) MTP with 1:1 whole blood:PRBC if component therapy unavailable, (3) FAST to exclude intra-abdominal source, (4) if there is ongoing hemorrhage despite binder — preperitoneal pelvic packing (PPP) in the OR, (5) external fixation (anterior frame or C-clamp) to reduce pelvic volume, (6) if still bleeding after PPP — internal iliac artery ligation (ligation of the anterior division of the internal iliac artery on the side of the fracture) as a salvage maneuver, (7) ICU resuscitation and second-look at 24-48h for pack removal.

operativeNuances.nuance: Preperitoneal pelvic packing (PPP) is performed through a low midline incision (Pfamemstiel or lower midline approach). The peritoneum is reflected superiorly to access the retropubic space (Retzius). Packs are placed on each side of the bladder, into the paravesical spaces, and then laterally into the pararectal spaces to compress the presacral venous plexus and iliac vessels. Up to 3 packs can be placed per side. The abdomen is closed temporarily. Packs are removed or changed in 24-48 hours. PPP is combined with pelvic external fixation (anterior frame or C-clamp) to reduce the pelvic volume and provide bony stability. The sequence: bind → external fixate → pack → angio if needed.

External fixation of the pelvis: there are two main types. (1) Anterior external fixator (AEF): place 5 mm Schanz pins in the iliac crest (one at the iliac tubercle, one 2 cm posterior, and one at the ASIS) or the supra-acetabular region (more stable). Connect with a carbon fiber or steel frame. The AEF provides rotational stability but limited posterior stability. (2) Pelvic C-clamp: applied posteriorly over the sacroiliac joints to compress the posterior pelvic ring. Useful for APC and VS patterns with posterior disruption. The C-clamp is placed with the patient supine — the pins are inserted percutaneously onto the posterior ilium at the level of the SI joint, 1 cm lateral to the posterior superior iliac spine. Both devices should be applied in the ED or OR under sedation or anesthesia.

Internal iliac artery ligation as a salvage maneuver: if PPP and external fixation fail to control hemorrhage and angioembolization is unavailable, ligation of the anterior division of the internal iliac artery on the side of the fracture can be performed. Approach: retroperitoneal dissection through a lower midline or Pfannenstiel incision. Identify the ureter (crossing the pelvic brim at the level of the sacroiliac joint), retract it medially. Identify the internal iliac artery (medial to the external iliac, divides into anterior and posterior divisions. Ligate the anterior division with 2-0 silk tie. Do NOT ligate the posterior division (which supplies the gluteal muscles and can cause buttock claudication and gluteal necrosis). Bilateral ligation should be avoided if possible due to the risk of bladder and rectal ischemia.

REBOA technique for pelvic hemorrhage (if available): obtain femoral artery access (ultrasound-guided) in the groin on the side without injury. Place a 7 Fr or 12 Fr sheath. Advance a compliant balloon catheter (e.g., ER-REBOA or CODA balloon) over a guidewire to zone 3 (infrarenal aorta, at the level of the aortic bifurcation L4-L5). Inflate the balloon with diluted contrast until femoral artery flow is lost on the opposite side. Occlusion time should be <30 minutes to minimize ischemic complications (AKI 20%, spinal cord ischemia <5%, lower extremity ischemia <5%). Deflate gradually and remove. REBOA is a BRIDGE to definitive hemostasis, not a definitive treatment.

Definitive fixation techniques: (1) Pubic symphysis plating — for APC injuries with diastasis >2.5 cm. Approach: Pfannenstiel incision, reduce the symphysis with a pointed reduction clamp, apply a 3.5 mm reconstruction plate (4-6 holes) with 2 screws on each side. (2) Iliosacral screw fixation — for posterior ring injuries (SI joint disruption or sacral fracture). Place the patient supine or prone. Under fluoroscopic guidance (inlet, outlet, and lateral sacral views), insert a 7.3 mm cannulated screw across the SI joint into the S1 vertebral body. (3) Anterior SI joint plating — direct open reduction and plating of the SI joint through a lateral approach. Timing: 5-7 days post-injury for DCO, at index surgery for ETC.

Damage control sequence for the open pelvic fracture: (1) Life-saving priorities — hemorrhage control first (PPP, external fixation), then contamination control. (2) Fecal diversion — perform a loop sigmoid colostomy with mucous fistula or Hartmann closure of distal limb. (3) Urinary diversion — suprapubic cystostomy tube for urethral injury. (4) Wound management — aggressive serial debridement (every 24-48h) of all non-viable tissue, copious irrigation, NPWT or open wound packing. (5) Definitive reconstruction — delayed primary closure or skin grafting once the wound is clean. (6) Antibiotics — 7-14 days of broad-spectrum coverage. The sequence must be followed strictly — fixing the pelvis before controlling contamination leads to deep pelvic sepsis.

The 'retroperitoneal hematoma at laparotomy': when performing a laparotomy for FAST-positive intra-abdominal hemorrhage in a patient with a pelvic fracture, a large retroperitoneal hematoma may be encountered. The rule: do NOT open a stable retroperitoneal hematoma (one that is not expanding, not pulsatile, and contained) secondary to pelvic fracture — opening it releases the tamponade and can cause uncontrollable hemorrhage. Indications to open a retroperitoneal hematoma: (1) expanding hematoma (suggests arterial source), (2) pulsatile hematoma (suggests iliac artery injury), (3) ruptured hematoma with free bleeding. If opened, control is by packing (PPP), direct vascular repair, or angioembolization.

In the Kenyan setting where pelvic external fixation frames may not be available, a pelvic 'sling' or 'wrap' technique using a bedsheet can serve as temporary stabilization. After PPP, wrap the sheet tightly around the pelvis at the level of the greater trochanters, cross it anteriorly, and tie it securely. Alternatively, use a 'pelvic C-clamp' improvised from a large towel clamp applied percutaneously to the posterior ilium. These temporizing measures can maintain pelvic reduction and provide enough stability for transport, ICU care, and eventual transfer to an orthopedic center.

prognosis.mortality: Unstable pelvic fracture with shock: 20-40%; isolated stable fracture: 2-5%
prognosis.kenyanMortality: Estimated 30-50% for unstable fractures due to transport delays, limited angiography, and limited blood products
prognosis.complicationRate: Hemorrhagic shock: 30-40%; ARDS: 10-20%; thromboembolism: 10-30%; wound infection (open fractures): 20-50%
prognosis.recoveryTimeline: ICU stay: 3-14 days; hospital stay: 2-6 weeks; full weight-bearing: 8-16 weeks; return to work: 4-12 months
prognosis.keyDeterminants: Hemodynamic stability at presentation, fracture pattern (Tile/Young classification), open vs closed fracture, associated injuries (especially head, chest, and genitourinary), and time to definitive hemorrhage control
prognosis.mortalityWithShock: Unstable pelvic fracture with hemorrhagic shock: 30-40% mortality; sustained hypotension >30 min despite transfusion increases mortality to 60%
prognosis.openPelvicMortality: Open pelvic fractures: 30-50% mortality due to combined hemorrhage and sepsis; fecal diversion (colostomy) reduces septic mortality
prognosis.complicationRates: Thromboembolism: 10-30% after pelvic trauma; DVT prophylaxis (LMWH + mechanical) essential; IVC filter if anticoagulation contraindicated
prognosis.guInjuryRates: Bladder rupture: 10-15% with pelvic fracture; urethral injury: 5-10% (more common in males); retrograde urethrogram before Foley insertion mandatory if blood at meatus
prognosis.neurologicInjury: Lumbosacral plexus injury: 5-15%; more common in sacral fractures with vertical shear; foot drop, perianal numbness, sexual dysfunction are common sequelae
prognosis.kenyanRecoveryChallenges: In Kenya, recovery is complicated by limited physiotherapy, poor orthopedic follow-up, high loss-to-follow-up, traditional bone setters delaying care, and lack of workplace accommodations — only 30% return to pre-injury employment within 12 months
prognosis.recoveryMilestones: ICU discharge: 3-7 days if isolated fracture; 7-21 days if polytrauma; hospital discharge: 2-6 weeks; partial weight-bearing: 6-8 weeks; full weight-bearing: 10-16 weeks; return to manual labor: 6-12 months
prognosis.qualityOfLife: Chronic pain (sacroiliac joint, pubic symphysis): 30-50%; gait abnormality: 20-30%; sexual dysfunction: 10-20%; chronic urinary symptoms: 10-15%
